lead: Furniture put into storage badly can come out worse than it went in, scratched, damp or smelling musty. Put away properly, it emerges months later just as you left it. Our Westerfield crews have stored Suffolk families' furniture since 2005, from heirloom wardrobes to leather sofas, and we've learned exactly what protects each kind and what ruins it. This guide walks you through preparing and storing furniture safely, whether it's going away for a few weeks between moves or settling in for the long haul.

sections.h: Clean Everything Before It Goes Into Store
sections.body:
The first rule of storing furniture is to put it away clean and bone dry. Anything stored with a trace of moisture, food or grime invites trouble over time, from mould and musty smells to marks that set permanently into the surface. A thorough clean before storage is the single best thing you can do to ensure your furniture comes out in the same condition it went in.

Wipe down wooden pieces with an appropriate cleaner and let them dry completely. Vacuum upholstered sofas and chairs, treat any small stains, and make sure cushions are fully dry before they go away. Leather wants a gentle clean and a conditioner to stop it drying and cracking during a long spell in store. Metal fittings can be wiped over to remove anything that might tarnish.

Crucially, give everything time to dry before it's wrapped, because sealing in even a little dampness is asking for mildew. In our experience, a rushed clean the night before is far less effective than tackling it a few days ahead so pieces can air out properly. It feels like extra effort at a busy time, but it's the step people are most grateful for when they open up clean, fresh furniture later.

Taking furniture apart where it's sensible to do so makes storage safer and far more space-efficient. Beds, tables with removable legs, bookcases and flat-pack units all travel and store better in pieces. Disassembled furniture stacks flat, takes up less room and is much less likely to be damaged than a bulky whole item that gets knocked or has weight leaned against it.

The golden rule when dismantling is to keep every screw, bolt and fitting together with the piece it belongs to. Pop the fixings into a clearly labelled resealable bag and tape it to the underside of the item or to a main panel, so it can't go astray. Nothing is more frustrating than reassembling a bed months later and finding the bolts have vanished. A quick photo on your phone before you take something apart makes putting it back together far easier too.

Glass shelves, mirrored doors and any detachable glass should come out and be wrapped separately as fragile items, never left in place where they can crack under pressure. If a piece is precious, antique or you're simply not confident taking it apart, leave it whole and let our crew handle the wrapping and moving. We'd rather take a bit more space than risk damaging something that means a lot to you.

sections.h: Wrap and Protect for the Journey and the Wait
sections.body:
How you wrap furniture decides whether it survives storage unscathed, and the material you choose matters more than people realise. For wooden and upholstered furniture, breathable covers such as proper furniture blankets or specialist covers are best, because they protect against knocks and dust while still letting the piece breathe. Avoid sealing solid wood tightly in plastic, which traps moisture against the surface and can cause warping or mildew over time.

Pad the corners and edges of wooden furniture, as these are what take the brunt of any contact. Cover the surfaces of tables and the tops of chests to guard against scratches, and slip mattresses and sofas into proper protective covers to keep them clean through a dusty store. Leather and delicate fabrics benefit from a soft layer underneath the outer cover so nothing rubs against the finish.

For longer storage especially, the breathability point is worth repeating, because the most common cause of furniture coming out damaged is being wrapped airtight in plastic and left to sweat. Use covers that let air move, and your furniture stays fresh. Our crews carry the right protective materials and know which wrap suits which piece, so if you're unsure, this is one job that's genuinely worth leaving to people who do it every week.

Once furniture is clean, dismantled and wrapped, how it's arranged in storage is the final piece of the puzzle. Heavier, sturdier items go on the bottom, with lighter and more delicate pieces on top, never the reverse. Stacking a heavy chest on a wrapped sofa will crush and mark it, so the order things go in genuinely matters for what comes out intact.

Don't lean heavy objects against the flat surfaces of wooden furniture, and avoid resting weight on the middle of shelves or tabletops where they can bow over time. Sofas can often be stood on end to save floor space, provided they're well wrapped and stable, which is a trick we use to make the most of a storage area without piling pressure on any one piece. Leave a little air space where you can rather than cramming everything tight.

The environment counts too. Furniture stores best somewhere clean, dry and away from extremes of damp and temperature, which is exactly what good storage provides. Keeping an inventory of what's gone in, and roughly where, saves a real headache if you need to retrieve one item later. Pack it thoughtfully and your furniture waits patiently, ready to come back out and slot straight into your new home whenever you're ready for it.

sections.h: Guarding Against Damp, Dust and Pests
sections.body:
The slow, invisible enemies of stored furniture are damp, dust and the occasional unwelcome visitor, and a little forethought keeps all three at bay. Damp is the chief culprit behind musty smells and mildew, which is why storing somewhere dry matters so much and why furniture should never go away even slightly wet. Breathable covers help air circulate, but the environment itself needs to be sound; furniture stored in a damp space will suffer however well it's wrapped.

Dust is more of a nuisance than a danger, but over months it dulls finishes and works into upholstery, so covering surfaces and slipping sofas and mattresses into proper covers keeps everything fresh. The covers also discourage anything from nesting in soft furnishings, which is the last thing you want to discover when a sofa comes out of store. Making sure nothing edible or organic is left inside drawers or cushions removes the temptation in the first place.

For longer stays especially, these small precautions pay off handsomely. Lift furniture slightly off a hard floor where you can, leave a little air space rather than cramming everything tight, and resist the urge to seal solid wood in plastic. Do these things and your furniture sits out its time in store quietly and emerges clean, dry and ready, with none of the unpleasant surprises that catch people out when storage is done in a hurry.

sections.h: Special Cases and How We Can Help
sections.body:
Some furniture needs that bit more thought than the average wardrobe. Antiques and genuinely valuable pieces deserve careful, breathable wrapping and a stable environment, since they're the items most affected by damp and temperature swings. Solid wood in particular dislikes being wrapped airtight, so these always go into store under breathable covers with their corners well protected.

Leather sofas and chairs want conditioning before storage and a soft, breathable cover, never plastic against the hide. Mattresses should be clean, dry and stored flat or carefully on edge inside a proper cover so they don't sag or pick up marks. Electronics built into furniture, and anything with delicate mechanisms like recliners, benefit from being handled gently and kept away from any risk of moisture.

This is where leaning on an experienced team pays off. Our crews prepare, wrap, move and store furniture as a single, careful process, using the right materials for each kind of piece, and your belongings are fully covered while they're in our care. We keep an inventory, store everything sensibly and deliver it back to your new home when you're ready, so there's no double handling and no worrying whether something was put away correctly. If you have a piece you're anxious about, just tell us and we'll give it the attention it deserves.

faqs: How do I prepare furniture before storing it?

Clean every piece thoroughly and let it dry completely, since storing anything damp invites mould and musty smells. Wipe wood, vacuum upholstery, condition leather and dry cushions fully. Give pieces a few days to air rather than rushing a clean the night before, so nothing goes into store carrying moisture.
Should I take furniture apart for storage?

Where it's sensible, yes. Dismantling beds, tables and flat-pack units saves space and reduces the risk of damage. Keep all screws and fittings in a labelled bag taped to the item, and photograph it before taking it apart to make reassembly easy. Leave precious or antique pieces whole if you're unsure.
What should I wrap furniture in for storage?

Use breathable covers such as furniture blankets, not airtight plastic, especially for solid wood, which can warp or grow mildew if moisture is sealed against it. Pad corners and edges, protect surfaces from scratches, and use proper covers for mattresses and sofas. Our crews carry the right materials for each type of piece.
How should furniture be arranged in storage?

Put heavy, sturdy items on the bottom and lighter, delicate pieces on top, never the reverse. Don't lean weight against flat wooden surfaces or rest it on the middle of shelves. Well-wrapped sofas can stand on end to save space. Keep an inventory so you can find any item without unpacking everything.
